choices.text: Prints the LAST field of each line, where NF is awk's built-in 'number of fields' variable — and `$NF` is 'the value of the field numbered NF' (i.e., the last one). Equivalent to 'print the column whose index = total columns.' Useful when the relevant data is at the end of variable-width lines (file paths, exit codes, the last item in space-separated output). Compare: `$1` is first, `$2` is second, `$NF` is last, `$(NF-1)` is second-to-last. The pattern generalizes: NR is line number, length(\$0) is line length, both available everywhere in awk programs. Knowing the built-in vars unlocks most of awk's everyday utility.

questions.explanation: $NF = the last field. NF is field count; $NF dereferences to that column's value.

questions.prompt: You have a CSV with millions of rows. Need to sum column 3 of rows where column 1 = 'US'. Tool?

choices.text: awk: `awk -F',' '$1 == "US" {sum += $3} END {print sum}' file.csv` — streams through the file in one pass, no memory blow-up regardless of size. CSV with millions of rows would crash or thrash Excel; awk handles it in seconds because it processes one line at a time without loading the whole file. Caveats: this assumes the CSV doesn't have embedded commas inside quoted fields (real-world CSV often does; for that, Python's csv module or csvkit's csvgrep are safer). For clean, simple CSV (no embedded commas in fields), awk is the workhorse. Pattern generalizes: any 'filter rows by column, aggregate another column' is awk's sweet spot.

questions.explanation: awk handles millions of rows in one pass: `awk -F, '$1=="US" {sum+=$3} END{print sum}'`.
